questions.question: Explain the difference between Access Control Lists (ACL) and Capability Lists as mechanisms for implementing protection. Discuss one advantage of each.
keyPoints: ACL associates rights with objects (column-wise implementation)
Capability lists associate rights with domains/subjects (row-wise implementation)
ACL advantage: Easy to revoke access by modifying object's list
Capability advantage: Easy to determine what a domain can access, supports delegation

questions.question: What is the Principle of Least Privilege? Why is it important in operating system protection?
keyPoints: Every user/process should have only minimum privileges needed
Limits damage from errors or malicious actions
Reduces attack surface
Prevents privilege escalation attacks
Example: root only when absolutely necessary

questions.question: Describe the Access Matrix model for protection. How can it be implemented efficiently?
keyPoints: 2D matrix with domains as rows and objects as columns
Each cell M[i,j] contains access rights of domain i on object j
Can be sparse - most entries are empty
Implementation options: Global table, ACL per object (columns), Capability lists per domain (rows)
Each implementation has trade-offs in space and time

questions.question: Explain the UNIX file permission mechanism with example. How does setuid bit work?
keyPoints: Three categories: owner, group, others
Three permissions each: read, write, execute (rwx)
Represented as 9 bits or 3-digit octal (e.g., 755)
setuid bit: executable runs with owner's UID, not caller's UID
Allows privilege elevation for specific programs (e.g., passwd)

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in OS protection?
keyPoints: Authentication: Verifying identity of user/process (who are you?)
Authorization: Determining what an authenticated user can do (what can you access?)
Examples: Password verification vs checking file permissions
Authentication happens first, then authorization

questions.question: Explain the concept of protection domain in operating systems. How does domain switching occur?
keyPoints: Domain = set of objects + access rights to those objects
Processes execute within protection domains
Domain switching: moving from one domain to another
Used for privilege escalation/de-escalation
Example: user process calling kernel via system call

questions.question: What are the two fundamental properties of the Bell-LaPadula security model?
keyPoints: Simple Security Property: No read up (cannot read higher classification)
Star Property (*-property): No write down (cannot write to lower classification)
Prevents information flow to lower security levels
Also called 'simple' and 'star' properties
Enforces confidentiality in multi-level security systems

questions.question: Discuss the various mechanisms for implementing protection in operating systems. Compare Access Control Lists and Capability Lists in terms of revocation, efficiency, and ease of use. Provide examples to illustrate.
keyPoints: Protection mechanisms: Access Matrix, ACL, Capabilities, Ring protection
ACL: Object-centered, easy revocation, hard to determine subject's rights
Capability: Subject-centered, supports delegation, hard revocation
Efficiency: ACL good for few users per file, Capability good for many files per user
Hybrid approaches exist (e.g., modern systems use both)
Examples: UNIX uses ACL-like permissions, some systems use explicit capabilities

questions.question: What are the security policies and mechanisms in UNIX/Linux operating systems? Explain how file permissions work and discuss the limitations of the traditional UNIX permission model.
keyPoints: Authentication: /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, PAM
Authorization: User IDs, Group IDs, file permissions
File permissions: owner/group/others with rwx bits, special bits (setuid, setgid, sticky)
Limitations: No fine-grained ACLs in traditional model (now extended with ACLs)
No user-defined groups beyond primary/group
No mandatory access control
Root bypasses all permissions
SELinux/AppArmor add mandatory controls

questions.question: Explain the concept of multilevel security. Discuss the Bell-LaPadula model and its properties. What are its limitations?
keyPoints: MLS: Objects and subjects have security classifications
Bell-LaPadula: Confidentiality-focused security model
Simple Security Property: No read up
Star Property: No write down
Discretionary Security Property: Access matrix handling
Limitations: Focuses only on confidentiality, not integrity
Does not address covert channels adequately
Difficult to implement in practice
Does not handle modern multi-category security needs
